points: Food security means availability, accessibility, and affordability of food to all people at all times.
Food insecurity affects landless labourers, traditional artisans, casual workers, SCs, STs, OBCs with poor land-base, women, and children under 5.
Natural calamities like drought cause food shortages, price rise, and can lead to famine—as seen in the Bengal Famine of 1943 that killed thirty lakh people.
Hunger has two dimensions: chronic (persistently inadequate diet) and seasonal (related to agricultural cycles). Both have declined significantly since 1983.
The Green Revolution (1960s-70s) made India self-sufficient in foodgrains, especially wheat and rice, helping avoid famines thereafter.
Total foodgrain production increased from 80 million tonnes (1960-61) to 315 million tonnes (2021-22).

summary.category: Institutional Framework: Buffer Stock and PDS
points: Buffer stock is foodgrain (wheat, rice) procured by FCI at Minimum Support Price (MSP) and stored in granaries to distribute in deficit areas.
Public Distribution System (PDS) distributes food through ration shops at subsidised Issue Prices to families with Antyodaya, BPL, or APL cards.
PDS evolved from universal coverage (pre-1992) to targeted systems: RPDS (1992), TPDS (1997), AAY and APS (2000), and NFSA (2013).
Challenges include excessive buffer stocks (leading to wastage and high carrying costs), over-concentration on wheat/rice in a few states, dealer malpractices, and declining APL participation.
Cooperatives like Amul (Gujarat) and Mother Dairy (Delhi) and NGOs like ADS Grain Banks (Maharashtra) play important roles in supplementing government efforts.
The National Food Security Act, 2013 provides legal entitlement to subsidised foodgrains for 75% of rural and 50% of urban population.
